topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. I reread the subject line three times, my eyes blurring slightly over the screen. Dr. Albright was leaving. The words swam before me, refusing to settle into any coherent meaning. I sat bolt upright in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my sudden tension. My hands, which had been hovering over my keyboard, clenched into tight fists on my desk. A slow, almost shaky breath escaped my lungs. What did this mean for the next six months? The entire structure of my research, the guidance I’d relied on, was suddenly tilting off its axis. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding deafening in the quiet library corner. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent campus quad, feeling a strange, hollow emptiness bloom in my chest.

[story 2]

"So, he's really going?" I managed to ask, the words sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. My voice cracked on the last syllable. Professor Davies had been the bedrock of my dissertation plan, the one person who truly understood the labyrinthine nature of my historical analysis. I leaned back in my worn desk chair, the springs protesting loudly. My gaze kept flicking toward the office door, a rigid posture settling over my shoulders. My mind was a frantic, buzzing hive of unanswered questions. I picked up my pen, but the ink seemed to smear as I tried to focus. A wave of dizziness washed over me; the fluorescent lights above seemed too bright, too harsh. I needed a moment, just a second to process the sheer magnitude of the shift happening right in front of me.

[story 3]

I stared at the screen, my jaw slack. It was a simple announcement, yet it felt seismic. Dr. Chen, my advisor, was departing the university. My fingers hovered over the mouse, trembling slightly before I finally clicked away, leaving the message unread. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. The sudden movement made me sway for a beat before I regained my balance. I walked over to the bookshelf, running my fingertips over the spines of old texts, seeking some anchor in the familiar. A cold, unsettling sensation prickled the back of my neck. The air in the room felt suddenly thin, and I couldn't seem to draw a full, steady breath. Everything felt unstable.
